title: Remove unused ioutils.HashData
body:
Related: https://github.com/podman-container-tools/podman/pull/29011

<!--- Please read the [contributing guidelines](https://github.com/containers/container-libs/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) before proceeding --->

comments.body: I would like to use `ioutils.NewAtomicFileWriter` in `quadlet` binary to avoid code duplication, but including the whole `crypto` library makes the binary significantly bigger. The function HashData is the only one using the `crypto` library in `ioutils` and the function is not used in either of `podman`, `buildah`, and `skopeo`.

> I would like to use ioutils.NewAtomicFileWriter in quadlet binary to avoid code duplication, but including the whole crypto library makes the binary significantly bigger. The function HashData is the only one using the crypto library in ioutils and the function is not used in either of podman, buildah, and skopeo.

A comment like that would make sense to put in the commit description instead of just the link and let people second guess.

What I don't like about the splitting is that we could find ourselves in a similar situation later, with `writer` for example instead, so we'd have to repeat the process again.

While with the current approach, `quadlet` has access to the whole library which may be potentially useful later to avoid another deduplication.

But if breaking the API is problematic, then I am fine with the splitting.

Or I'm thinking, perhaps we could move the `HashData` into a different package instead? So that if somebody wants to use it, they still can, just with different import.

> What I don't like about the splitting is that we could find ourselves in a similar situation later, with `writer` for example instead, so we'd have to repeat the process again.

If we ended up with `pkg/ioutils/[40 subpackages]`, that’s… fine? Tedious, sure, but that’s one-time tedium and machines can help.

(All of this is unfortunate in that `crypto/sha256` has an `init()` function and that drags in various dependencies; generally Go is fairly good at removing unreferenced code within an imported subpackage.)

> Or I'm thinking, perhaps we could move the `HashData` into a different package instead?

That would still be API break — if we were fine with that, it would be easier to remove the code if we don’t know about any users.

> > What I don't like about the splitting is that we could find ourselves in a similar situation later, with `writer` for example instead, so we'd have to repeat the process again.

> (All of this is unfortunate in that `crypto/sha256` has an `init()` function and that drags in various dependencies; generally Go is fairly good at removing unreferenced code within an imported subpackage.)

A slightly tongue-in-cheek alternative would be to create an `pkg/ioutils2` which contains _everything_ but `HashData`, and have `pkg/ioutils` mostly consist of forwarders, expecting that Go can optimize out the rest. I think that would be much more work upfront, _and_ we’d lose the opportunity to clean up things like `SetDefaultOptions` (pretty unclean and was dropped by the intended user).

> If we ended up with pkg/ioutils/[40 subpackages], that’s… fine? Tedious, sure, but that’s one-time tedium and machines can help.

Even if so, I think that's better for efficiency, since the idea for this is `let's not rewrite code which is in container-libs already`, not necessarily that `we need atomic file writer for quadlets`. The manually written code for my use case was longer by about 15 lines maybe? After that, who knows if somebody will need it again. If we prepare the package now, someone else needing any of the functionalities later can just use the functions and not do PR to container-libs with another splitting, waiting for merge, then vendoring container-libs,...

The size difference would be negligible, right? Given that there is no other big dependency like `crypto`. `ioutils` now increases the quadlet binary size by 1144 bytes.

> A slightly tongue-in-cheek alternative would be to create an pkg/ioutils2 which contains everything but HashData, and have pkg/ioutils mostly consist of forwarders, expecting that Go can optimize out the rest. I think that would be much more work upfront, and we’d lose the opportunity to clean up things like SetDefaultOptions (pretty unclean and was dropped by the intended user).

I think that these kind of decisions are getting out of scope of what I wanted to achieve in the first place.  And I don't know how justifiable it is to potentially introduce a bit of a mess unless we have a clear idea about what we actually need to have in the package to be reused in quadlets? So considering just my PR, my honest opinion would be either dropping `HashData` or using my own implementation of atomic writing. But if we decide that we want some of these bits of refactoring, I am fine with doing it.

(I‘m not quite sure what you are advocating for in the comment above. Is that splitting `pkg/ioutils` into 40 subpackages _immediately_?)

> > If we ended up with pkg/ioutils/[40 subpackages], that’s… fine? Tedious, sure, but that’s one-time tedium and machines can help.
> 
> If we prepare the package now, someone else needing any of the functionalities later can just use the functions and not do PR to container-libs with another splitting, waiting for merge, then vendoring container-libs,...

I think the quadlet binary might be fairy rare in being in the containers ecosystem and never computing a `sha256` digest; and being built for an environment where size matters so much.. I’m not saying _unique_, but… maybe close? Validating digests is required for processing any on-registry images, for example.

Also… parts of the code have been inherited from a ~2016-era codebase. `HashData` is by no means the only function with no _known_ caller. So I would advocate not proactively migrating _everything_ — if something has no users, just let it rot [and perhaps truly drop it if it is later proven to have been 100% broken for years, without anyone noticing].

> The size difference would be negligible, right? 

vs. which alternative do you mean?

> If we ended up with `pkg/ioutils/[40 subpackages]`, that’s… fine?

To be clear, I‘m not at all saying that this PR should do 40 subpackages. I was thinking split the one subpackage you care about; and _if_ we ever need to split one or more in the future for similar reasons, do that at that time.

> (All of this is unfortunate in that crypto/sha256 has an init() function and that drags in various dependencies; generally Go is fairly good at removing unreferenced code within an imported subpackage.)

Well IMO that is what makes go not good, the use of init() in all sorts of packages causes these problems. And then also there is the other variant with dead code elimination not working properly when used with reflect. I.e. all function receivers will be included even when they never get called via reflect as of course reflect is runtime code the compiler cannot optimize out.

---

Regarding API breakage I did a query on sourcegraph which finds no matches when I exclude vendor/ files.  So IMO lets not overthink this and just remove it. If someone somewhere depends on it they can complain to us and we add it back and restructure packages.

https://sourcegraph.com/search?q=context:global+HashData%28+-file:vendor/+lang:go+content:go.podman.io/storage/pkg/ioutils&patternType=keyword&sm=0

> vs. which alternative do you mean?

I got confused in terminology a bit, sorry. I meant that hypothetically, `ioutils` could grow to be a bigger package than it is now, which could reintroduce the problem again.

The way I see it, removing `HashData` is simple and we get not only atomic writer, but also all the other functions that are/will be there. So that we don't need to do PR with splitting to `container-libs` every time we realize we need something, which is in the spirit of avoiding code duplication.

We can decide to do the refactoring later on, whether it is because somebody complains about missing `HashData` or any other reason.

Either way, I don't think I want to spend more time on this or be responsible for what this causes. I have the code without atomic writer ready, this was just Paul's idea to reuse the container-libs code.

> Well IMO that is what makes go not good, the use of init() in all sorts of packages causes these problems. And then also there is the other variant with dead code elimination not working properly when used with reflect.

Good reasons to use very small subpackages.

---

> I meant that hypothetically, `ioutils` could grow to be a bigger package than it is now, which could reintroduce the problem again.

The callers that care and moved to use the new smaller subpackages would not be affected.

Also, an important part of the ~point of doing that now is to create a _precedent_ of having subpackages of `pkg/ioutils`, to decrease a likelihood of such an addition in the future.

We have _learned_ something about `pkg/ioutils` today… it would be fine to preserve it for the future generations.

---
 
> Either way, I don't think I want to spend more time on this or be responsible for what this causes. I have the code without atomic writer ready, this was just Paul's idea to reuse the container-libs code.

Uh, that rather motivates me to move to the Quadlet PR and block it on achieving a clean solution :|

“I don’t want to do this super cleanly and I’d rather add one more copy” is a bad habit to get into. _You_ might very well be the one maintaining the two copies in the future (and wondering why one has a `fsync` and the other doesn’t and …).

And it _must not_ be a way to get a PR merged.

Let’s avoid that habit, please.

----

> Regarding API breakage I did a query on sourcegraph

Fair enough. It’s by no means the worst API break we did over the year. Let’s get this in as is, then.
I added more context to the commit message.
